## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

Shared palette across all diagrams: blue = client apps, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey = datastores, purple = external.

### 6.1 "Rider gets a fare estimate for A→B"

The rider needs a price and ETA before committing. This slice introduces the read path into the live market and the routing/ETA dependency.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **API Gateway** | The front door that terminates TLS, checks the JWT, and routes requests | One place for auth, rate-limiting, and routing so services stay simple | Not per-service auth (duplicated logic, easy to get wrong) | One more hop (~20ms) on every request |
| **Estimate Service** | A stateless service that computes distance, ETA, and applies surge | Pricing logic in one place, scales horizontally | Not in the client (client can't be trusted with price) | Needs live surge data, so it reads the surge store |
| **Routing/ETA Service** | External maps engine that turns two points into distance+time | Building our own routing is years of work; treat ETA as a call | Not Haversine straight-line (ignores traffic, roads — wrong) | External dependency; we cache and degrade if it's slow |
| **Surge Store (Redis)** | In-memory map of geo-cell → current surge multiplier | Surge changes by the minute; needs a fast read keyed by cell | Not a SQL table (read-per-estimate at 1K/s is wasteful) | Surge is eventually consistent (seconds stale — fine) |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **API Gateway** — the single entry point that authenticates and routes every request. Here it lets every backend service trust that the caller is already identified.
> **Surge multiplier** — a number like 1.4 that multiplies the base fare when demand outstrips supply in an area. Here it's read live per estimate so the quote reflects the current market.
> **ETA** — estimated time of arrival, computed by a routing engine over the road network and traffic. Here it both prices the ride and tells the rider how long until pickup.

**Diagram — fare estimate read path:**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Rider app]:::client
    R -->|"1 POST estimate"| GW[API Gateway]:::svc
    GW -->|"2 forward"| EST[Estimate Service]:::svc
    EST -->|"3 distance and ETA"| RT[Routing ETA Service]:::ext
    EST -->|"4 read surge for cell"| SG[(Surge Store Redis)]:::store
    EST -->|"5 quote with estimateId"| GW
    GW -->|"6 200 quote"| R
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ede9fe
```

**Numbered narration:**
1. Rider POSTs pickup+dropoff. *Why gateway:* it owns auth so the Estimate Service never re-checks the token. *State:* none yet — read-only. *What if it fails:* gateway returns 401/429; rider retries.
2. Gateway forwards to Estimate Service. *Why:* pricing is centralized server-side. *State:* none. *What if:* gateway load-balances to a healthy replica.
3. Estimate Service asks Routing for distance+time. *Why:* roads and traffic, not straight lines. *State:* none. *What if Routing is slow:* fall back to a cached/region-average ETA and flag the quote as approximate.
4. It reads the current surge for the pickup cell. *Why Redis:* surge is hot and changes by the minute. *State:* none. *What if surge store is down:* default to 1.0x (no surge) — fail open on price.
5. It returns a quote with an `estimateId`. *State:* the quote is briefly cached so the request can pin it. *What if:* if nothing pins it, it just expires.
6. Rider sees "~$15, 4 min."

> 💬 "The key insight here: the price is computed server-side from live surge, and we hand back an id so the price the rider saw is the price we honor."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "For an estimate, the rider posts the two points, the gateway authenticates, and the Estimate Service combines a real routing call for distance and time with a live surge multiplier read from Redis. I deliberately don't price in the client — that'd be trivially hackable — and I treat routing as an external service with a cached fallback so a slow maps call degrades to an approximate quote instead of a spinner. We hand back an estimate id to pin the price."

### 6.2 "Rider requests a ride and we match a nearby driver"

This is the heart. The rider commits; we find a nearby available driver from the live geo-index. New components: the Trip Service (state machine), the Matching Service, and the live Geo-Index.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Trip Service** | Owns the Trip state machine and the durable trip record | Money/state correctness needs one authoritative owner with transactions | Not letting matching write trips directly (no single owner ⇒ races) | A hop between match and trip persistence |
| **Matching Service** | Stateless worker that reads candidate drivers for a cell and ranks them | Matching is a read-scan + score; horizontally scalable per region | Not matching inside the geo-index (mixing storage with logic) | Must read the index, so it's co-located by region |
| **Geo-Index (Redis, sharded by cell)** | In-memory store of every online driver's position, keyed by geo cell | ~1.1M writes/s + sub-30ms "who's near me" — only RAM survives this | Not Postgres/PostGIS (can't take a million writes/s); not S3 (not real-time) | Best-effort, ephemeral — a lost ping waits 4s |
| **Geocell library (S2/geohash)** | A scheme that maps a lat/lng to a small grid cell id | Lets "near me" become "same cell + 8 neighbors" — a key lookup, not a distance scan | Not raw lat/lng range scans (slow, no clean sharding key) | Cell edges mean we scan neighbors too |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Geo-Index** — an in-memory map from geo cell to the drivers currently in it. Here it answers "which drivers are near this pickup" in milliseconds without scanning a database.
> **S2 / geohash** — schemes that encode a latitude/longitude into a short string/integer for a grid cell. Here they turn a proximity search into a fast key lookup on a cell and its neighbors.
> **State machine** — a model where an entity can only move between defined states (MATCHING → MATCHED → EN_ROUTE → COMPLETED). Here it guarantees a trip can't skip or repeat a stage.

**Diagram — match flow:**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Rider app]:::client
    R -->|"1 POST trip"| GW[API Gateway]:::svc
    GW -->|"2 create trip MATCHING"| TS[Trip Service]:::svc
    TS -->|"3 persist trip"| TDB[(Trip Store)]:::store
    TS -->|"4 match request"| MQ[[Match Queue]]:::async
    MQ -->|"5 dequeue"| MS[Matching Service]:::svc
    MS -->|"6 read cell and neighbors"| GI[(Geo-Index Redis)]:::store
    MS -->|"7 atomic offer to driver"| GI
    MS -->|"8 offer created"| TS
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```
↳ reuses existing: API Gateway (from §6.1)

**Numbered narration:**
1. Rider POSTs the trip with the estimateId + idempotency key. *State:* none yet. *What if double-tapped:* idempotency key returns the same trip.
2. Gateway routes to Trip Service. *Why Trip Service:* it's the single owner of trip state.
3. Trip Service writes a trip in `MATCHING`. *Why persist first:* if matching crashes, the trip still exists and a sweeper can retry. *State:* durable trip created. *What if DB write fails:* return 503; rider retries with same key.
4. Trip Service enqueues a match request. *Why a queue:* decouples the fast 202 ack from the few-second match; absorbs bursts. *State:* request buffered.
5. A Matching Service worker dequeues it. *Why a worker:* matching is CPU-light but I/O-bound on the index; scale workers independently.
6. Worker reads the pickup cell + 8 neighbors from the geo-index. *Why neighbors:* the nearest driver might be just across a cell edge. *State:* none (read). *What if cell is empty:* widen the radius (next ring of cells) before giving up.
7. Worker picks the best candidate and **atomically flips** them to `offered` (compare-and-set). *Why atomic:* prevents offering one driver to two riders. *State:* driver soft-locked. *What if the CAS loses:* try the next candidate.
8. Offer recorded; Trip Service is notified to create the MatchOffer. *State:* trip now has a pending offer.

> 💬 "The whole trick of matching is turning 'who's near me' into 'who's in my cell and the eight around it' — a key lookup, not a database scan — and the offer is an atomic flip so no driver gets double-offered."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When a rider requests, the Trip Service creates a trip in MATCHING and persists it first — that way a crash can't lose the request — then drops a match job on a queue. A Matching worker reads the pickup's geo cell plus its eight neighbors from the in-memory index, which is fast because S2 cells turn proximity into a key lookup. It ranks the candidates and atomically flips the best one to 'offered' with a compare-and-set, so two riders can never grab the same driver. The trade-off is the index is best-effort and ephemeral, but for live positions that's exactly right — a stale ping just gets corrected in four seconds."

### 6.3 "Driver accepts the offer; we lock the pairing"

The offer is a soft lock with a timeout. This slice adds the Dispatch channel to the driver and the accept transition.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dispatch / Push channel** | A persistent connection (WebSocket) to the driver app to deliver offers instantly | Offers are time-sensitive (15s); push beats polling | Not polling (adds latency + load for an event-driven need) | Must manage long-lived connections + reconnects |
| **Offer timeout sweeper** | A background timer that expires unanswered offers and re-matches | Drivers miss offers; we must release the soft lock automatically | Not relying on the driver to decline (they may just ignore it) | A timer wheel / delayed queue to track expiries |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **WebSocket** — a persistent two-way connection between app and server. Here it pushes the offer to the driver instantly and carries the accept back.
> **Soft lock with timeout** — a temporary reservation that auto-releases if not confirmed. Here it holds a driver for one rider for ~15s, then frees them.

**Diagram — accept / re-match:**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MS[Matching Service]:::svc
    MS -->|"1 push offer"| DP[Dispatch Channel]:::svc
    DP -->|"2 deliver to driver"| D[Driver app]:::client
    D -->|"3 POST accept"| GW[API Gateway]:::svc
    GW -->|"4 atomic confirm"| TS[Trip Service]:::svc
    TS -->|"5 trip MATCHED"| TDB[(Trip Store)]:::store
    TS -->|"6 driver on-trip"| GI[(Geo-Index Redis)]:::store
    SW[Timeout Sweeper]:::async -.->|"7 expire if no accept"| MS
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```
↳ reuses existing: Matching Service, Trip Service, Geo-Index, Trip Store, API Gateway

**Numbered narration:**
1. Matching pushes the offer to the Dispatch channel. *State:* offer pending, driver soft-locked.
2. Dispatch delivers to the driver's app over the live connection. *What if driver is offline/disconnected:* offer times out fast, re-match immediately.
3. Driver taps accept → POST. *Object authz:* JWT driver id must equal the offer target.
4. Gateway routes to Trip Service for an atomic confirm. *Why atomic:* the offer must still be PENDING and unexpired. *What if expired:* 409, driver told "offer expired."
5. Trip moves to `MATCHED`. *State:* durable pairing recorded.
6. Driver flips to `on-trip` in the index so no one else matches them. *State:* driver removed from the available pool.
7. If no accept within ~15s, the sweeper expires the offer and triggers a re-match. *Why:* prevents a rider waiting forever on a silent driver.

> 💬 "An offer is a reservation with a fuse — if the driver doesn't accept in fifteen seconds it auto-releases and we re-match, so nobody's stuck."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The offer goes to the driver over a persistent push connection because fifteen seconds matters. They accept, and the Trip Service does an atomic confirm — only valid if the offer's still pending — then marks the trip MATCHED and flips the driver to on-trip so they leave the matchable pool. The piece people forget is the timeout sweeper: if the driver ignores the offer, a background timer releases the soft lock and re-matches, so a silent driver never strands a rider."

### 6.4 "Both track the trip live on a map"

Now the firehose becomes useful: the driver's position streams to the one rider watching. New component: the Location Ingest path + the track fan-out.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Location Ingest Service** | Stateless service that takes the driver ping firehose, updates the index, and emits to the track stream | Decouples the write firehose from everything else | Not writing pings straight to the trip DB (would melt it) | One extra hop on each ping (cheap, stateless) |
| **Kafka (location stream)** | A durable log that buffers location events for downstream consumers | Lets analytics, ETA refresh, and history consume pings without touching the hot path | Not direct calls to each consumer (couples producer to all) | Pings in Kafka are best-effort-durable, async |
| **Track Pub/Sub** | A channel keyed by trip that pushes the matched driver's position to the rider | Fan-out of one driver to exactly one rider watching | Not broadcasting all positions to all clients (privacy + waste) | Connection state per active trip |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Kafka** — a durable, append-only log that buffers a firehose of events so slow consumers don't lose data. Here it carries location pings to history, ETA, and analytics off the hot path.
> **Pub/Sub** — publish/subscribe: producers publish to a topic, subscribers get the messages. Here the rider subscribes to their trip's topic to receive the driver's live position.

**Diagram — live track:**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Driver app]:::client
    D -->|"1 location pings"| GW[API Gateway]:::svc
    GW -->|"2 forward firehose"| LI[Location Ingest]:::svc
    LI -->|"3 update live position"| GI[(Geo-Index Redis)]:::store
    LI -->|"4 emit event"| KF[[Kafka location stream]]:::async
    LI -->|"5 push to trip topic"| PS[Track PubSub]:::svc
    PS -->|"6 stream to rider"| R[Rider app]:::client
    KF -.->|"7 consume async"| HIST[(Location History)]:::store
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```
↳ reuses existing: API Gateway, Geo-Index

**Numbered narration:**
1. Driver app streams pings (every ~4s). *State:* none durable.
2. Gateway forwards to Location Ingest. *Why a dedicated service:* isolate the firehose so it can't drag down matching or trips.
3. Ingest overwrites the driver's position in the geo-index. *Why overwrite:* only the latest matters for matching/tracking. *What if a ping is lost:* next one arrives in 4s — within our 5s freshness SLO.
4. Ingest emits the event to Kafka. *Why:* history, ETA refresh, and analytics consume it without touching the hot path. *What if Kafka is slow:* ingest keeps serving live updates; only the async consumers lag.
5. For drivers on an active trip, Ingest pushes to that trip's track topic. *State:* none durable; transient delivery.
6. Rider's open stream receives the position; map updates. *What if rider disconnected:* reconnect resumes from last known position.
7. Kafka consumers persist breadcrumbs to Location History (the ~58 TB/mo table) asynchronously.

> 💬 "Tracking is just the firehose, filtered: the driver's ping updates the index for matching AND gets pushed to the one rider watching this trip — same event, two jobs."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The driver's location pings hit a dedicated Ingest service so the firehose is quarantined from everything correctness-critical. Ingest does three things with each ping: overwrites the live position in Redis for matching, emits it to Kafka for history and ETA, and — if the driver's on a trip — pushes it to that trip's topic so the rider's map moves. Losing a ping is a non-event because another comes in four seconds, which is inside my freshness target."

### 6.5 "Compute the fare and charge the rider"

The trip ends; we compute the real fare from the actual route and charge exactly once. New component: the Payment Service + the saga.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Payment Service** | Owns charging the rider and paying the driver, idempotently | Money needs an isolated owner with strong consistency + idempotency | Not charging inside Trip Service (mixes concerns, harder to audit) | A hop + a saga between trip-complete and paid |
| **Trip Store (SQL, sharded by trip_id)** | The durable trip + fare record with transactions | A finished trip + fare is a row that must commit atomically | Not Cassandra for the trip (no strong multi-field txn for money) | Manual sharding by trip_id |
| **Payment processor** | External (Stripe) that does the actual card charge | We don't want PCI scope; processors are battle-tested | Not handling cards ourselves (PCI nightmare, liability) | External latency + occasional outages to handle |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Saga** — a sequence of steps with compensating undo actions, used instead of one big distributed transaction. Here it coordinates trip-complete → charge → payout so a failure can be cleanly undone.
> **Idempotency key** — a unique token (here the trip id) that makes a repeated request produce the same single result. Here it guarantees a retry never double-charges.

**Diagram — fare + payment:**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Driver app]:::client
    D -->|"1 POST complete"| GW[API Gateway]:::svc
    GW -->|"2 finalize trip"| TS[Trip Service]:::svc
    TS -->|"3 compute fare from route"| RT[Routing ETA Service]:::ext
    TS -->|"4 trip COMPLETED + fare"| TDB[(Trip Store)]:::store
    TS -->|"5 charge event"| PAY[Payment Service]:::svc
    PAY -->|"6 charge idempotent on tripId"| PROC[Payment Processor]:::ext
    PAY -->|"7 record payment"| TDB
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ede9fe
```
↳ reuses existing: API Gateway, Trip Service, Trip Store, Routing/ETA

**Numbered narration:**
1. Driver marks the trip complete. *State:* trip ending.
2. Trip Service finalizes. *Why it owns this:* it's the trip's authoritative state machine.
3. It computes the real fare from the actual driven route (distance + time + surge locked at request). *Why server-side:* the fare must be derived, never client-claimed.
4. Trip moves to `COMPLETED` with the fare, committed atomically. *State:* durable, billable.
5. Trip Service emits a charge event to Payment. *Why async/saga:* the processor can be slow or down; don't block trip completion on it.
6. Payment charges the processor **idempotently on tripId**. *What if it times out:* retry with the same key — the processor returns the original charge, never a second.
7. Payment records the result. *What if charge fails (declined):* mark trip `PAYMENT_FAILED`, retry/dunning later — the ride still completed.

> 💬 "Money is the one place I refuse to be eventual: the fare's computed server-side from the real route, and the charge is keyed on trip id so a retry is the same charge, never a double."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When the driver completes, the Trip Service computes the fare from the actual route — never trusting any client number — commits the trip as COMPLETED, and hands a charge event to a dedicated Payment Service. Payment charges the external processor idempotently on the trip id, so if the network hiccups and we retry, the processor returns the same charge instead of billing twice. I run this as a saga rather than a distributed transaction because the processor is external — I can't hold a lock across it — so on a decline I mark the trip payment-failed and reconcile, without ever holding up trip completion."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

### PART A — Entity table

| Entity | Key fields | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rider | **rider_id (PK)**, name, payment_method_token, active_trip_id | SQL (sharded) | rider_id | Strong |
| Driver | **driver_id (PK)**, vehicle, status, rating | SQL (sharded) | driver_id | Strong |
| DriverLocation | **driver_id (key)**, geo_cell, lat, lng, heading, status, ts | Redis (geo-sharded) | geo_cell prefix | Best-effort, last-write-wins |
| Trip | **trip_id (PK)**, rider_id, driver_id, status, fare, route_summary, ts_history | SQL (sharded) | trip_id | Strong |
| MatchOffer | **offer_id (PK)**, trip_id, driver_id, status, expires_at | Redis (TTL) | trip_id | Strong (atomic CAS) |
| Payment | **payment_id (PK)**, trip_id (idempotency key), amount, status | SQL (sharded) | trip_id | Strong |
| LocationHistory | **(trip_id, ts) (PK)**, driver_id, lat, lng | Object store + OLAP, time-partitioned | day partition | Eventual |

### PART B — Storage decision cards

**🗄️ Redis (in-memory) — used for: DriverLocation (Geo-Index), Surge Store, MatchOffer**
1. **What it is:** Redis is an in-memory key-value store — data lives in RAM so reads and writes are sub-millisecond, with optional geospatial commands.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** the location firehose is ~1.1M writes/s with sub-30ms "who's near me" reads; only RAM survives that throughput, and ~540 MB of live positions fits comfortably. Its geo/sorted-set support makes cell+neighbor lookups native, and atomic CAS makes the offer soft-lock safe.
3. **What we considered and rejected:**
   - **Postgres + PostGIS** — beautiful geo queries, but a single node can't take a million writes/s; we'd be sharding it into oblivion and still fighting disk.
   - **Cassandra/DynamoDB** — great write throughput, but a disk LSM-tree write per ping is wasteful when we only ever need the latest, and proximity reads aren't native.
   - **Elasticsearch geo** — query-rich but ingest can't keep up with this write rate, and it's heavier to operate.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** the index is ephemeral — a Redis failover can lose recent positions, but drivers re-ping within 4s so the index self-heals.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Live driver positions go in Redis, in memory, because we're doing about a million writes a second and we only ever care about the latest position. Postgres can't take that write rate, and a disk database is wasteful when every value is overwritten in four seconds. The risk is a failover loses some positions, but they come right back from the next ping."

**🗄️ SQL (sharded Postgres) — used for: Rider, Driver, Trip, Payment**
1. **What it is:** a relational database that stores rows with strict schemas and guarantees a multi-step operation either fully commits or fully rolls back (ACID transactions).
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** money and trip state must be strongly consistent and atomic — completing a trip and recording its fare can't half-commit, and a payment must be exactly-once keyed on trip_id. Only a transactional relational store gives that cleanly.
3. **What we considered and rejected:**
   - **DynamoDB** — fine for simple KV, but the trip+fare+payment lifecycle wants multi-row transactions and rich queries Dynamo makes painful.
   - **MongoDB** — flexible schema, but its transactions are less battle-tested for money than Postgres.
   - **MySQL** — essentially equivalent; Postgres is the modern default with better JSON and partitioning.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** manual sharding by trip_id; cross-shard joins are impossible so we denormalize the rider/driver summary onto the trip.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Trips and payments go in sharded Postgres because it's money — I need atomic commits and exactly-once charging. Dynamo's great for the geo-index but I don't want to hand-roll transactional logic for the money path. The cost is I shard by trip id and can't do cross-shard joins, so I denormalize."

**🗄️ Kafka — used for: location event stream (feeds history, ETA refresh, analytics, surge)**
1. **What it is:** a durable, append-only log that buffers a firehose of events so multiple consumers can read at their own pace without losing data.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** location events have many independent consumers (history, surge, ETA, fraud, analytics) and we must decouple them from the hot path; Kafka fans one stream out durably.
3. **What we considered and rejected:**
   - **Direct service calls** — couples the producer to every consumer and loses events if one is down.
   - **AWS Kinesis / Pub/Sub** — viable managed equivalents; Kafka chosen for partition control and ecosystem maturity.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** another system to operate; events are async so consumers can lag.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I put location events on Kafka so history, surge, and analytics each consume the firehose at their own speed without touching the live path. If one consumer falls behind, the log holds the events; it doesn't take down matching."

**🗄️ Object store + OLAP (S3 + warehouse) — used for: LocationHistory**
1. **What it is:** cheap, durable blob storage (S3) plus an analytics warehouse for querying it.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** ~58 TB/mo of breadcrumbs are write-once, read-rarely (disputes, routing improvement, analytics) — cheap durable storage beats keeping it in an operational DB.
3. **What we considered and rejected:**
   - **Keeping it in Postgres/Redis** — orders of magnitude too expensive and would crush the operational store.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** querying history is slow (warehouse-speed), which is fine — it's not on any hot path.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Raw GPS history is sixty terabytes a month, written once and read almost never, so it goes to cheap object storage with a warehouse on top for the occasional dispute or analytics query."

### PART C — Per-operation consistency

| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why that level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Create trip / accept offer | SQL + Redis CAS | Strong | A driver can't be double-booked; trip state must be authoritative |
| Charge payment | SQL | Strong, exactly-once | Money — idempotent on trip_id |
| Read driver position (track) | Redis | Eventual (≤5s) | A few seconds stale is invisible and fine |
| Read surge for estimate | Redis | Eventual (seconds) | Market drifts; a slightly old multiplier is acceptable |
| **Read-your-own-writes: rider sees their new trip** | SQL primary | Strong | After POST /trips returns, the track stream reads trip from primary (not a lagging replica) so the rider always sees MATCHING immediately |

### PART D — Data lifecycle (LocationHistory, the biggest table)

At ~1.9 TB/day of breadcrumbs, a 90-day retention window caps us at ~175 TB steady-state — without it we'd grow unbounded. We store it **time-partitioned by day**, and reclaim space with a **partition DROP** (an O(1) instant operation that removes a whole day's table) rather than row-by-row DELETE (which churns indexes and never cleanly reclaims space). Data older than 90 days that must be kept for compliance is rolled to **Glacier-tier cold storage**; everything else is dropped.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Storage splits cleanly by guarantee. The money and trip data is in sharded Postgres for atomic, exactly-once correctness — I considered Dynamo but didn't want to hand-roll transactional logic for charges. Live positions are in Redis because we're doing a million writes a second on data we overwrite constantly. Kafka carries the location firehose to all the async consumers so nothing slow touches the hot path. And the big sixty-terabyte breadcrumb history sits in cheap object storage with day-partitions I just drop after ninety days."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the bottleneck — "a million location writes a second, fastest in the densest cities" — and reason forward from there.

### How do we absorb the location-update write firehose (~1.1M writes/s)?

**#### Bad: write every ping to Postgres**
- 🗣️ *Plain words:* just store each GPS update as a row in the main database.
- **Approach:** every ping is an `UPDATE driver_location SET lat=...` in Postgres.
- *Why people try it:* it's one store, one mental model.
- ⚠️ *What breaks:* at ~1.1M writes/s, a Postgres node handling maybe ~10-20K writes/s is overwhelmed 50-100x over; the WAL and connection pool saturate in seconds and the whole DB — including trips and payments — goes down with it.
- 🔁 *Forces upgrade:* the firehose volume itself; we need an in-memory store, not disk.

**#### Good: in-memory geo-index sharded by cell**
- ↩️ *What the previous tier got wrong:* Bad put a million disk writes/s on a transactional DB. Good moves live positions to RAM and overwrites instead of appending.
- 🗣️ *Plain words:* keep every driver's current spot in fast memory, bucketed by map cell, and just overwrite it each ping.
- **Approach:** a Redis cluster keyed by **geo cell** (S2/geohash). Each ping is a key overwrite (last-write-wins). We **geo-shard**: cell-prefix → shard, so ~11+ shards each handle ~100K writes/s. Pings are best-effort — no durability on the hot path; they're mirrored to Kafka async for history.
- ⚠️ *What breaks:* a single dense downtown cell pins one shard — ~500 writes/s plus the read-scan load all on one node — so a uniform-hash assumption is wrong; traffic is geographically lumpy.
- 🔁 *Forces upgrade:* the hot dense-city cell — one shard becomes a hotspot for both writes and the 18K-row read scan.

**Mechanism diagram (Good):**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Driver pings]:::client
    D -->|"1 ping by cell"| LI[Location Ingest]:::svc
    LI -->|"2 hash cell to shard"| RT{Shard router}:::svc
    RT -->|"3 cell A"| S1[(Geo shard 1)]:::store
    RT -->|"4 cell B"| S2[(Geo shard 2)]:::store
    RT -->|"5 cell C"| S3[(Geo shard N)]:::store
    LI -.->|"6 async mirror"| KF[[Kafka]]:::async
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```

**#### Great: adaptive cell sizing + sub-sharding hot cells**
- ↩️ *What the previous tier got wrong:* Good used fixed-size cells, so a dense cell overloads one shard while a rural cell is nearly empty.
- 🗣️ *Plain words:* make the map grid finer where it's crowded and coarser where it's empty, and split a hot cell across several machines.
- **Approach:** use **hierarchical cells** (S2 levels) — downtown Manhattan splits into many small cells, rural areas use one big cell. A cell that exceeds a write/read threshold is **sub-sharded** (its drivers spread across replicas by a secondary key). Reads scan the sub-shards in parallel and merge top-K. Cell sizing adapts to driver density, rebalanced periodically.
- 🔢 *Decision math:* a fixed level-13 cell downtown = ~2,000 drivers, ~500 writes/s + 18K-row scans on ONE node. Splitting into 8 finer cells = ~250 drivers and ~60 writes/s each — back under any single-node concern.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Shard node dies | Drivers in that cell re-ping within 4s; index rebuilds; matching briefly widens radius |
| Hot cell overloads | Auto sub-shard triggered at threshold; reads fan out and merge |
| Ping lost | Next ping (≤4s) corrects; within freshness SLO |
| Rebalance mid-traffic | Dual-read old+new cell during cutover so no driver is invisible |
| Whole region down | Failover to standby region; pings re-establish in seconds |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The firehose is the whole ballgame — a million writes a second. I refuse to put that on a disk database; it goes into an in-memory Redis index keyed by geo cell, where each ping just overwrites the last because I only care about the latest position. I shard by cell so the load spreads, but the catch is cities are lumpy — one downtown cell can pin a single shard. So I use adaptive cell sizes — fine grid where it's dense, coarse where it's empty — and I sub-shard any cell that gets too hot, fanning the read scan across nodes and merging the top candidates."

> 🧠 *If they ask "why not just buy bigger Redis nodes?":* "Vertical scaling tops out and doesn't fix the hotspot — one dense cell is on one node no matter how big. Geographic sharding plus sub-sharding hot cells is the only thing that spreads a lumpy load."

### How do we match efficiently in a hot dense city (the celebrity cell)?

**#### Good: scan cell + neighbors, rank top-K**
- 🗣️ *Plain words:* look at the requester's cell and the eight around it, score the drivers, pick the best.
- **Approach:** read candidate drivers from the 9 cells, score by ETA (not straight-line distance), offer the top one.
- ⚠️ *What breaks:* downtown, those 9 cells hold ~18K drivers — scanning and scoring all of them per request, with ~100/s match requests funneling to that one cell-shard, makes the dense city the bottleneck.
- 🔁 *Forces upgrade:* the read-scan cost + concurrent matchers contending on the same hot cell.

**#### Great: bounded top-K with concurrent-offer guarding**
- ↩️ *What the previous tier got wrong:* Good scanned all 18K and let concurrent matchers fight over the same drivers.
- 🗣️ *Plain words:* keep drivers pre-sorted by location so we grab just the nearest handful, and make sure two simultaneous requests don't both grab the same driver.
- **Approach:** maintain drivers in a **sorted geo structure** (Redis GEO sorted-set) so we read only the nearest ~20 candidates, not all 18K. Each offer is an **atomic CAS** flipping the driver to `offered`; a concurrent matcher that loses the CAS moves to the next candidate. Sub-shard the hot cell (from the firehose dive) so the scans parallelize.
- 🔢 *Decision math:* scanning 20 sorted candidates vs 18,000 = ~900x less work per match; at 100 matches/s on the hot cell that's the difference between feasible and melted.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Two riders, one driver | First CAS wins; second matcher skips to next candidate |
| All nearby drivers busy | Widen to next ring of cells, then queue rider with surge nudge |
| Driver goes offline mid-offer | Offer times out; sweeper re-matches |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "In a dense city, naively scanning every nearby driver is thousands of rows per request. Instead I keep drivers in a sorted-by-distance structure so I only pull the nearest twenty. The other half is concurrency: two requests downtown might eye the same driver, so the offer is an atomic compare-and-set — first one wins, the loser just takes the next-best. Combined with sub-sharding the hot cell, the densest block stays fast."

> 🧠 *If they ask "why ETA not distance?":* "Straight-line distance ignores a river or a one-way street — the closest car as the crow flies might be 15 minutes away. I score by routing ETA so the offer is actually the soonest pickup."

### How do we keep payments exactly-once across a crash?

**#### Bad: charge synchronously inside trip completion**
- 🗣️ *Plain words:* charge the card in the same step that ends the trip.
- ⚠️ *What breaks:* if the processor times out, we don't know if it charged; a retry might double-bill, and holding the trip transaction open across a 300ms external call exhausts connections.
- 🔁 *Forces upgrade:* the external call can't be inside a DB transaction.

**#### Great: saga with idempotency key = trip_id**
- ↩️ *What the previous tier got wrong:* Bad coupled the commit to an external charge it can't control.
- 🗣️ *Plain words:* finish the trip first, then charge separately with a unique receipt number so a retry can't double-bill.
- **Approach:** trip completion commits in Postgres independently. A **saga** then charges the processor with `tripId` as the **idempotency key** — the processor dedupes, so a retry returns the original charge. On decline, mark `PAYMENT_FAILED` and run dunning; never re-run the trip.
- 🔢 *Decision math:* with synchronous charging, 1K completions/s × 300ms processor latency = 300 in-flight DB transactions just waiting on the network — pool exhaustion. The saga frees the DB the instant the trip commits.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Charge succeeds, our record crashes | Reconcile by trip_id; processor shows charged; we record it; no double-charge |
| Charge times out | Retry same key; processor returns original result |
| Card declined | Trip stays COMPLETED, payment PAYMENT_FAILED, dunning retries |
| Duplicate complete event | Idempotent on trip_id; second is a no-op |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "I never charge inside the trip transaction — the processor is external and slow. I commit the completed trip in Postgres, then a saga charges with the trip id as the idempotency key, so any retry returns the same charge instead of billing twice. If the card declines, the ride still completed; I just mark payment-failed and run dunning. And if our side crashes after the processor charged, I reconcile by trip id — the processor is the source of truth for whether money moved."

> 🧠 *If they ask "why not two-phase commit?":* "We don't own the processor, so we can't make it a transaction participant. A saga with idempotency is the standard way to coordinate across a system you don't control."

### How does surge pricing work (the demand/supply deep dive)?

**#### Good: per-cell supply/demand ratio**
- 🗣️ *Plain words:* if a neighborhood has way more riders than drivers, raise the price there.
- **Approach:** per geo cell, compute `open_requests / available_drivers` over a short window; map the ratio to a multiplier; write it to the Surge Store. Estimates read it.
- ⚠️ *What breaks:* a raw instantaneous ratio is jumpy — surge flickers 1.0→2.5→1.2 minute to minute, which feels arbitrary and triggers gaming.
- 🔁 *Forces upgrade:* volatility and the feedback loop (high surge pulls drivers in, which should lower it smoothly).

**#### Great: smoothed, hysteresis-banded surge with feedback**
- ↩️ *What the previous tier got wrong:* Good's raw ratio whipsawed riders and drivers.
- 🗣️ *Plain words:* average the demand over a rolling window and only change the price when it crosses clear bands, so it moves smoothly.
- **Approach:** an **exponentially-weighted moving average** of the supply/demand ratio per cell, mapped to multiplier **bands with hysteresis** (must cross a threshold to step up, a lower one to step back down) so it doesn't flicker. Drivers see a heatmap, pull toward surge, which naturally relaxes it — a closed feedback loop computed by a stream job consuming the Kafka location+request events.
- 🔢 *Decision math:* a 2-minute EWMA over events smooths a spike that a 10-second window would amplify; hysteresis bands (e.g. step up at 1.5x ratio, down at 1.2x) prevent oscillation around a boundary.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Surge pipeline lags | Estimates use last-known multiplier (stale-but-safe), default 1.0 if absent |
| Sudden event (concert lets out) | EWMA ramps over ~2 min, not instant spike; bands cap the jump |
| Gaming (drivers log off to spike) | Smoothing + minimum-supply floor dampens manipulation |

**Mechanism diagram (Great):**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    KF[[Kafka events]]:::async
    KF -->|"1 requests and supply"| SJ[Surge Stream Job]:::svc
    SJ -->|"2 EWMA per cell"| CALC[Banded multiplier]:::svc
    CALC -->|"3 write multiplier"| SG[(Surge Store)]:::store
    SG -->|"4 read at estimate"| EST[Estimate Service]:::svc
    CALC -.->|"5 heatmap"| D[Driver app]:::client
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Surge is just per-cell supply versus demand, but the naive ratio flickers and feels arbitrary. So a stream job consumes the request and location events from Kafka, keeps a smoothed moving average per cell, and maps it to multiplier bands with hysteresis so the price only steps when demand clearly crosses a threshold. Drivers see a heatmap and drift toward the surge, which relaxes it — a feedback loop. If the pipeline lags, estimates just use the last-known multiplier, defaulting to no surge, so we fail safe on price."

> 🧠 *If they ask "what if surge is wrong/stale?":* "It's eventually consistent by design — a few seconds stale is fine, and we pin the rider's quote with an estimate id so whatever they saw is what they pay, even if it changes a second later."

### How do we handle back-pressure when the system is overloaded?

**#### Great: bounded queues + load-shedding order**
- 🗣️ *Plain words:* when we're drowning, drop the least important work first and protect the money path.
- **Approach:** the Match Queue and Kafka are **bounded**; the trigger signal is **consumer lag / queue depth**. Under overload we shed in order: (1) downgrade location freshness (ping every 8s not 4s) → (2) reject new estimate reads with 429 → (3) shed non-critical analytics consumers → never shed trip completion or payment. Clients get **HTTP 429 with Retry-After**.
- 🔢 *Decision math:* doubling the ping interval halves the firehose from ~1.1M to ~550K writes/s — instant relief on the binding bottleneck without dropping any trip.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Match queue depth spikes | Shed reads first; widen ping interval; protect accepts/payments |
| Kafka consumer lag grows | History/analytics lag (acceptable); hot path unaffected |
| Total overload | Load-shed reads, return 429+Retry-After; money path stays up |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Under overload I shed work in a deliberate order. First I widen the GPS ping interval — going from four seconds to eight instantly halves the firehose, my biggest load, while the map's barely less fresh. Then I start rejecting estimate reads with a 429 and a Retry-After. The one thing I never shed is trip completion and payment — the money path is sacred. The queues are all bounded, and queue depth is the trigger, because an unbounded queue is just a slow-motion outage."

> 🧠 *If they ask "why widen pings first?":* "It's the cheapest, least-visible relief — it attacks the single biggest load source and a rider can't tell the difference between a 4-second and 8-second map refresh."

---

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

### 9A — Availability targets

- **Matching path: 99.9% ("three nines")** = ~8.7 hours/year max downtime. Mechanisms: stateless services behind a load balancer, **multi-AZ** *(multiple data centers in one region so if one building loses power another takes over automatically)*, Redis with replicas + fast failover. We accept three nines here because a brief matching blip is recoverable — the rider retries.
- **Payment/trip path: 99.99% ("four nines")** = ~52 min/year. Mechanisms: synchronously-replicated Postgres (RPO≈0), idempotent charging so retries are safe, multi-AZ with automatic failover.
- **Location track: best-effort** — a few seconds of staleness is acceptable; no strict nines because a lost ping self-corrects.

### 9B — Per-dependency failure table

| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what the USER sees) | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geo-Index (Redis) | Can't match / track | Matching pauses briefly; riders see "finding a driver"; drivers re-ping and index rebuilds in seconds | Failover to replica; index rebuilt from incoming pings |
| Trip Store (Postgres) | Can't create/complete trips | New requests fail (503, retry); in-flight trips tracked from cache; money frozen, not lost | Multi-AZ failover, synchronous replica promoted |
| Matching Service | No new matches | Riders wait in MATCHING; existing trips track fine | Stateless — restart/scale replicas |
| Payment processor | Can't charge | Trip completes normally; charge queued and retried later | Saga retries; dunning; reconcile by trip_id |
| Routing/ETA | No fresh ETAs | Estimates use cached/region-average ETA; quote flagged approximate | Circuit-breaker + cache; auto-recover |
| Kafka | History/surge/analytics lag | Live matching & tracking unaffected; surge uses last-known | Consumers catch up from the durable log |

### 9C — RPO / RTO per data class

> **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we can lose if it crashes right now. RPO≈0 means none — every write is replicated before we acknowledge. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until service is back.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Payments / Trips | ≈0 (no loss) | < 1 min | Synchronous multi-AZ replication; auto-failover |
| DriverLocation (live) | seconds (lossy OK) | seconds | Self-heals from next ping; no DR needed |
| LocationHistory | minutes (rebuildable) | hours OK | Kafka replay re-populates; cold tier durable |
| Surge state | seconds (recomputed) | seconds | Stream job recomputes from Kafka |

### 9D — Cost breakdown

Top 3 drivers:
1. **The location write tier (Redis cluster + Kafka + ingest):** ~$200K/mo — the firehose dominates, working out to **~$0.21 per 1M location updates**. This, not the trip DB, is the biggest line item.
2. **Location-history storage + warehouse:** ~58 TB/mo of breadcrumbs; storage itself is cheap (~$2K/mo on object store) but the warehouse compute for queries adds up.
3. **Egress / push connections:** millions of long-lived track + dispatch connections cost in bandwidth and connection-handling fleet.

**Biggest optimization:** the ping interval. Widening from 4s to 8s roughly halves the firehose — the single largest cost — at a barely-perceptible freshness cost. Adaptive ping rate (fast when on a trip, slow when idle) captures most of the savings without hurting active rides.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Here's what users actually feel when things break. If the geo-index hiccups, riders briefly see 'finding your driver' and drivers re-ping their way back in seconds — nothing lost. If Postgres fails over, in-flight trips keep tracking from cache and money is frozen, never lost, because payments are synchronously replicated at RPO zero. If the payment processor is down, the ride still ends normally and we charge a bit later via the saga. The one thing that's expensive is the firehose itself — about twenty cents per million pings — and the cheapest big win is simply pinging less often when a driver's idle."

---

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: in-memory best-effort geo-index vs durable geospatial DB**
1. **Chose & why:** Redis in RAM — only thing that absorbs ~1.1M writes/s with sub-30ms reads.
2. **Gave up:** durability of live positions — a failover can lose recent pings.
3. 🗣️ *Plain words:* like a whiteboard you wipe and rewrite constantly instead of a logbook — fast, but it doesn't remember history.
4. **Reverses when:** if regulators require every breadcrumb durably on the hot path, I'd switch to a log-structured durable ingest and ~5-10x the write-tier cost.
5. 🗣️ *How to say it:* "Live positions are disposable, so I keep them in fast memory and let a lost ping self-correct in four seconds."

**🔀 Decision: saga with idempotency vs distributed transaction for payment**
1. **Chose & why:** saga — we don't own the external processor, can't make it a transaction participant.
2. **Gave up:** the simplicity of a single atomic commit; we need reconciliation logic.
3. 🗣️ *Plain words:* like checking out at a store where the card machine is a separate company — you finalize the receipt, then run the card, and reconcile if the machine hiccups.
4. **Reverses when:** if we owned payments in-house, a real two-phase commit becomes possible.
5. 🗣️ *How to say it:* "I charge after committing the trip, keyed on trip id, so a retry can't double-bill and I never hold a lock across an external call."

**🔀 Decision: best-effort eventual track vs strongly-consistent location**
1. **Chose & why:** eventual (≤5s) — a near-live map is indistinguishable from live and infinitely cheaper.
2. **Gave up:** perfectly real-time positions.
3. 🗣️ *Plain words:* the car on your map is a couple seconds behind reality — which you'd never notice.
4. **Reverses when:** never realistically; sub-second would multiply cost for no user benefit.
5. 🗣️ *How to say it:* "Tracking is eventual on purpose — five seconds stale is invisible, and chasing real-time would blow the budget."

**CAP/PACELC framing:** The location/matching path is **AP** — under a partition I favor availability (serve possibly-stale positions, keep matching) over consistency. The payment/trip path is **CP** — I'd rather reject a write than risk a double-charge. PACELC: even without a partition, the location path favors **latency** over consistency, while the money path favors **consistency**.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The three decisions I'd defend hardest: live positions in memory and best-effort, because the firehose makes durability there absurdly expensive and a lost ping self-heals; a payment saga instead of a distributed transaction, because we don't own the processor; and eventual tracking, because nobody can tell a five-second-old map from a live one. The thread through all of them is the same — I'm AP and latency-first on the real-time geo path, and CP and consistency-first on money. The location decision reverses if regulators force durable breadcrumbs; the saga reverses if we bring payments in-house."

---

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two riders request at the same time and the nearest driver is the same person — who gets them?"**
*Mechanism:* matching offers a driver by an atomic compare-and-set (CAS) that flips them from `available` to `offered`; the first request wins the CAS, the second sees the driver already offered and moves to the next candidate.
🗣️ *Plain words:* whoever's request grabs the driver first locks them; the other request just picks the next-closest car.
💬 "Atomic CAS on the driver status — first to flip wins, no double-booking."

**❓ "What happens when Redis (the geo-index) crashes?"**
*Mechanism:* matching pauses momentarily; we fail over to a replica, and because every online driver pings every 4 seconds, the index rebuilds itself within seconds from incoming pings — we never persisted it as the source of truth.
🗣️ *Plain words:* the live map briefly goes quiet, then refills on its own as cars keep reporting in.
💬 "The index self-heals — drivers re-ping it back to life in seconds."

**❓ "How do you handle a flash crowd — a concert lets out and 10,000 people request at once?"**
*Mechanism:* the match queue absorbs the burst, the dense cell auto-sub-shards so the read scans parallelize, and surge ramps via a smoothed EWMA to pull in nearby drivers; under extreme load we widen ping intervals and shed estimate reads with 429s, never trip/payment.
🗣️ *Plain words:* we queue the spike, spread the hot neighborhood across more machines, and let surge draw drivers over.
💬 "Bounded queue + hot-cell sub-sharding + surge feedback — shed reads, protect rides."

**❓ "Can the same rider be charged twice for one trip?"**
*Mechanism:* the charge is idempotent on trip_id — the payment processor dedupes by that key, so a retry returns the original charge rather than creating a second.
🗣️ *Plain words:* the trip id is the receipt number; charging twice with the same number just returns the first charge.
💬 "Idempotency key = trip id, so a retry is the same charge, never a double."

**❓ "Can a rider see their trip immediately after requesting (read-your-own-writes)?"**
*Mechanism:* after POST /trips commits, the track stream reads the trip from the SQL primary (not a lagging replica), so the rider always sees `MATCHING` right away.
🗣️ *Plain words:* right after you tap request, we read your trip from the authoritative copy so you instantly see "finding a driver."
💬 "Read from primary right after the write — no replica-lag confusion."

**❓ "What if a driver's phone loses signal in a tunnel mid-trip?"**
*Mechanism:* the live position simply stops updating; the map shows the last known spot and an estimate; when signal returns the next ping snaps it forward — the trip state machine is unaffected because trip state lives in Postgres, not in the ping.
🗣️ *Plain words:* the car freezes on the map for a moment, then jumps to its real spot when signal's back; the trip itself never breaks.
💬 "Pings are best-effort; the trip lives in the DB, so signal loss is cosmetic."

**❓ "How do you stop someone from hacking a cheaper fare?"**
*Mechanism:* the client never sends a price — the server computes the fare from the actual route and the surge pinned by the estimate id; we re-validate the estimate server-side and ignore any client-supplied amount.
🗣️ *Plain words:* the price comes from our servers based on the real trip, not from anything the app sends, so there's nothing to tamper with.
💬 "Price is server-derived from the route and pinned estimate — the body can't set it."

**❓ "How do you make sure driver A can't accept driver B's offer or watch someone else's trip?"**
*Mechanism:* object-level authorization — we check the JWT's identity against the offer's target driver and the trip's rider/driver on every mutating and streaming endpoint.
🗣️ *Plain words:* we check not just that you're logged in, but that this specific offer or trip is actually yours.
💬 "Object-level authz on every endpoint — your id must own the resource."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part of this system?"**
*Mechanism:* the location write tier — Redis cluster + Kafka + ingest absorbing ~1.1M writes/s — at about $0.21 per million pings, dwarfing the trip database.
🗣️ *Plain words:* it's not storing trips, it's the constant flood of GPS updates from every car.
💬 "The firehose, about twenty cents per million pings — way more than the trip DB."

**❓ "How would you cut costs without hurting users?"**
*Mechanism:* adaptive ping rate — pinging every 8s when idle and 4s on a trip roughly halves the firehose, the dominant cost, with no perceptible freshness loss; partition-drop retention caps history growth.
🗣️ *Plain words:* have idle cars report in less often — you'd never notice, and it halves our biggest bill.
💬 "Adaptive ping intervals halve the firehose for a freshness drop nobody can see."

**❓ "Why straight-line distance isn't good enough for matching — what do you use?"**
*Mechanism:* we rank candidates by routing ETA, not Euclidean distance, because a river or one-way street can make the geographically-closest car the slowest to arrive.
🗣️ *Plain words:* the closest car on the map might be across a river — we pick the one that can actually get to you fastest.
💬 "Rank by real ETA, not crow-flies distance."

**❓ "How would you add UberPool (shared rides) later?"**
*Mechanism:* pooling makes matching a batched route-overlap optimization rather than greedy 1:1 — I'd add a windowed batch matcher that groups riders with compatible routes and assigns one driver to several, layered on the same geo-index.
🗣️ *Plain words:* instead of one rider one driver, we'd briefly wait, group people heading the same way, and send one car for all of them.
💬 "Add a windowed batch matcher on top of the geo-index — same index, different matching policy."

**❓ "How do you prevent an offer from stranding a rider if the driver ignores it?"**
*Mechanism:* every offer is a soft lock with a ~15s TTL; a timeout sweeper expires unanswered offers, releases the driver, and triggers a re-match automatically.
🗣️ *Plain words:* if a driver doesn't answer in fifteen seconds, we free them up and find someone else.
💬 "Offers auto-expire in 15s and re-match — no silent driver strands a rider."

**❓ "How do you make matching consistent across a region with multiple shards?"**
*Mechanism:* trips are region-local by assumption, and the geo-index is sharded by cell within a region; a request reads its cell + neighbors, which may live on a couple shards but always within one region — no cross-region coordination on the hot path.
🗣️ *Plain words:* a ride stays inside one region, and we only ever look at nearby cells, so there's no global coordination needed.
💬 "Region-local trips + cell-neighbor reads — no global coordinator on the match path."

**❓ "What if the matching service crashes after creating the trip but before matching?"**
*Mechanism:* the trip was persisted in `MATCHING` first, and the match request sits durably on the queue; a stuck-trip sweeper re-enqueues any trip in MATCHING past a timeout, so no request is silently lost.
🗣️ *Plain words:* the trip's already saved and the match job's on a queue, so if a worker dies another picks it up.
💬 "Persist trip first, match job on a durable queue, sweeper retries stuck ones."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "A ride-sharing service is, in plain words, a system that finds you a nearby car fast, lets you watch it come, and charges you the right amount once. The design splits into two halves. The real-time half is a firehose of GPS pings — about a million writes a second — landing in an in-memory geo-index keyed by map cell, where 'who's near me' becomes a fast cell-plus-neighbors lookup; the same pings stream to the rider's map and to Kafka for history. The transaction half is the trip state machine and payments in sharded Postgres — the hardest problem there is charging exactly once across an external processor, which I solve with a saga keyed on the trip id so a retry never double-bills. The two halves connect at the geo-index: matching reads what the location ingest writes, and Kafka carries everything async to history and surge. Every hard decision traces back to one principle — keep the moving-data path in memory and best-effort for speed, and keep the money path in SQL and exactly-once for correctness."
